topics.title: Structure of the Skin
bullet_points: The skin is the largest external and overall organ of the human body, covering an area of approximately 1.5 to 2 sqmtrs.
It acts as the primary barrier against environmental hazards, regulates body temperature, enables touch sensations, and constitutes about of total body weight.

Skin is composed of three primary layers: the outermost epidermis (waterproof barrier, skin tone), the middle dermis (contains connective tissue, hair follicles, sweat glands), and the deepest hypodermis (subcutaneous fat and connective tissue).
These layers protect, regulate temperature, and enable sensation.

topics.title: Layers of the Skin
bullet_points: 1. Epidermis (Outer Layer)
This layer acts as a barrier and is made of stacked epithelial cells, primarily keratinocytes, with no blood vessels. It contains melanocytes (color) and protects against environmental elements
Sublayers: From outside to inside, these include the stratum corneum, stratum lucidum (only in palms/soles), stratum granulosum, stratum spinosum, and stratum Basale
Keratinocytes produce keratin, structural scleroprotein that forms hair and nails.
Melanocytes impart colour by producing melanin pigment.
2. Dermis (Middle Layer)
Located beneath the epidermis, this layer is composed of collagen and elastin, providing strength and flexibility.
Components: It contains sweat glands, sebaceous (oil) glands, hair follicles, nerves, and blood vessels.
Regions: Divided into the upper papillary region and the lower, thicker reticular region.
3. Hypodermis (Subcutaneous Layer)
The deepest layer, also known as the subcutaneous tissue, consists mainly of adipose tissue (fat) and connective tissue.
Functions: It attaches the skin to muscle and bone, acts as a shock absorber, and helps conserve body heat

topics.title: Sweat Glands
bullet_points: Sweat glands, also known as sudoriferous or sudoriparous glands, are small tubular exocrine glands in the skin that secrete sweat, primarily to regulate body temperature through evaporative cooling.

Eccrine Glands: Located all over the body (dense on palms, soles, head), these secrete a watery, salt-rich fluid for cooling. Usage: Actively cooling the body during exercise, physical exertion, or in hot environments.
Apocrine Glands: Located in the armpits and genital areas, these secrete a thicker fluid into hair follicles. Usage: Activated by emotional stress or puberty-related hormonal changes.

topics.title: Sebaceous glands
bullet_points: Sebaceous glands are microscopic, exocrine organs in the skin that produce and secrete an oily, waxy substance called sebum. They are usually connected to hair follicles—forming the pilosebaceous unit—and are found over most of the body, particularly on the face and scalp, to protect and lubricate the skin while maintaining moisture.
Langerhans cells: Belong to the skin immune system.
Merkel cells: Responsible for tactile skin sensation.
Arrector pili muscle- Gives goosebumps

topics.title: Structure of the Nail
bullet_points: The human nail unit is a complex structure primarily composed of hard keratin, designed to protect fingertips and toes. Key components include the visible nail plate (the hard, translucent surface), the underlying nail bed, the matrix (which produces the nail), and surrounding skin folds (proximal/lateral folds) that seal and anchor it, ensuring protection and supporting dexterity.
Key Components of the Nail Structure
Nail Plate (Corpus Unguis): The hard, visible, and keratinized part of the nail, which sits on the nail bed, growing from the matrix.
Nail Bed: The soft tissue underneath the nail plate, rich in blood vessels and nerves, which gives the nail its pink color.
Nail Matrix (Root): Located under the proximal nail fold, this is the area where the nail plate originates and grows through cell division.
Lunula: The white, half-moon shaped area at the base of the nail, representing the visible part of the matrix.
Cuticle (Eponychium): A thin layer of dead skin that covers the proximal edge of the nail plate, acting as a seal against bacteria.
Proximal Nail Fold: The skin that borders the base of the nail, anchoring it.
Hyponychium: The area of thickened skin located under the free edge of the nail, sealing the nail bed from infection.
Free Edge: The part of the nail plate that extends beyond the finger or toe.
Lateral Nail Folds (Sidewalls): The skin folds on the sides that frame and protect the nail plate

topics.title: Structure of the hair follicle
bullet_points: Hair is a complex, keratinized fiber structured into three main layers—the cuticle, cortex, and medulla—anchored by a follicle, root, and bulb within the skin. The cuticle provides protection, the cortex gives strength and color, and the medulla forms the central core, all fueled by sebum for lubrication.
Hair Shaft (Visible Part): Comprised of three concentric layers.
Cuticle: The outermost protective layer, consisting of overlapping keratin scales similar to shingles on a roof. It protects the inner layers and accounts for roughly 20% of the hair's strength.
Cortex: The thickest middle layer containing keratin filaments, which provide elasticity and strength. It also holds melanin granules that determine hair color.
Medulla: The innermost core, often absent in fine hair. it is known to be porous and contain air spaces.
Hair Root (Under the Skin): Located inside the follicle.
Hair Follicle: A tube-like structure in the skin that anchors the hair and determines its growth direction and shape.
Hair Bulb: Located at the base of the root, containing dividing cells that produce new hair, which then become keratinized as they move up.
Sebaceous Gland: Produces sebum (natural oil) to coat and protect the hair shaft.

topics.title: Disease conditions of the Integumentary System
bullet_points: Acne (Acne Vulgaris): Caused by clogged hair follicles, sebum buildup, and bacteria, often leading to pimples, blackheads, and cysts.
Atopic Dermatitis (Eczema): A common chronic condition causing dry, itchy, red skin, often affecting children, which can crack or weep fluid.
Fungal Infections: Conditions like ringworm, athlete’s foot, and yeast infections thrive in warm, moist areas of the body.
Psoriasis: An immune-mediated disease-causing rapid skin cell turnover, resulting in thick, red, scaly patches that can be itchy or painful.
Viral Warts: Small, grainy skin growths caused by the Human Papillomavirus (HPV)
Rosacea: A chronic inflammatory condition causing facial redness, visible blood vessels, and sometimes pimple-like bumps.
Hives (Urticaria): Red, itchy, raised bumps or welts that appear on the skin, often as an allergic reaction.
Contact Dermatitis: Redness or rash caused by direct contact with a substance (e.g., allergies to soaps, metals, or plants).
Vitiligo: A condition where skin loses melanin, resulting in pale or white patches, often on the face, hands, or neck.
Skin Cancer (e.g., Basal Cell Carcinoma): Abnormal, uncontrolled growth of skin cells often caused by UV exposure, requiring medical treatment.
